title: A Snake as Long as a School Bus? Meet Vasuki, the 50-Foot Giant!
summary: Have you ever seen a snake and thought, "Whoa, that's big"? Now imagine one as long as a school bus — about 50 feet! That's the size of a newly discovered ancient snake called Vasuki indicus. Scientists found its fossils in a mine in Gujarat, India. This giant lived around 47 million years ago, back when the world was a very different place. The snake's backbone pieces, called vertebrae, were huge — some as wide as a grown-up's hand! By studying these bones, researchers think Vasuki was between 36 and 50 feet long. That makes it one of the biggest snakes ever, right up there with Titanoboa, another monster snake. But Vasuki was probably slow and sneaky. Like today's anacondas, it likely hid and waited to ambush its prey. The name Vasuki comes from a giant serpent in Hindu stories, which is a perfect fit for such a colossal creature. This snake belonged to an ancient family called madtsoiids that slithered around the world for almost 100 million years. Scientists think these mega-snakes first appeared in India and then spread to Europe and Africa. Finding Vasuki helps us understand how snakes evolved and how big they could really get. Who knows what other giant fossils are still buried, waiting to be discovered?
why_it_matters: This discovery shows that our planet was once home to creatures almost too big to imagine — and it may inspire you to explore nature or become a scientist who finds the next giant fossil!

keywords.explanation: Fossils are the rocky remains of ancient plants or animals that have turned into stone over millions of years.
Vertebrae are the small bones that link together to make up an animal's backbone.
Titanoboa was an even earlier giant snake that lived in South America and could grow over 40 feet long.
To ambush means to hide and suddenly attack prey that doesn't see you coming.
Colossal means something is extremely large, like a skyscraper or a whale.
Madtsoiids were an ancient family of snakes that lived on Earth for nearly 100 million years before going extinct.

questions.question: How long was Vasuki indicus, according to the scientists?
Where were the Vasuki fossils discovered?
options: In a mine in Gujarat, India
questions.correct_answer: In a mine in Gujarat, India

questions.question: What is the name of the other giant snake mentioned in the article?
How do scientists think Vasuki hunted?
options: By chasing prey at high speed
By ambushing prey like modern anacondas
questions.correct_answer: By ambushing prey like modern anacondas

questions.question: What do researchers hope to learn from studying Vasuki?
options: How snakes evolved and how big they could get
How to build longer school buses
questions.correct_answer: How snakes evolved and how big they could get

questions.question: Which ancient family did Vasuki belong to?

background_read: Many huge prehistoric animals once roamed the Earth, but giant snakes are rare finds. Titanoboa (a giant snake that lived in Colombia around 60 million years ago) was the record-holder until discoveries like Vasuki indicus came along.
Gujarat (a state in western India known for rich fossil sites) has been a treasure trove for paleontologists, revealing how life looked when India was still an island continent.
The name Vasuki comes from Hindu stories (ancient tales from Hinduism, a major religion in India) — Vasuki was a giant serpent king, often shown wrapped around the neck of the god Shiva.
Article_Structure: WHO: Scientists and researchers who discovered the fossils of an ancient snake named Vasuki indicus.
WHAT: The discovery of a fossil of one of the largest snakes ever — possibly up to 50 feet long — that lived 47 million years ago.
WHERE: In a mine in Gujarat, India.
WHY: To help us understand how snakes evolved over millions of years and how enormous they could become.

perspectives.description: Paleontologists are thrilled because Vasuki fills a gap in snake evolution and proves India played a key role in spreading these mega-snakes across continents.
Some researchers note that only backbones were found, so the exact length is still a guess — Vasuki might have been shorter or longer than the current estimate.
Naming this snake after a Hindu serpent king adds a cultural twist, showing how science and ancient stories can intersect.
